Collection › Song Dynasty › ro-song-008 — Guan Yao Vase with Dragon Handles

Reference Object ro-song-008

Guan Yao Ribbed Vase with Dragon-Ear Handles

官窑 — Southern Song Dynasty, 12th–13th century CE — Laohudong / Jiaotanxia, Hangzhou

Southern Song Guan Ware Dragon Handles Grey-Green Celadon Glaze Banding Zi Kou Tie Zu Anorthite Wenrun Ruyu GOM Examined

Object Description

A large ribbed vase with a tall trumpet mouth, globular ribbed body, paired dragon-ear handles with ring loops, and a narrow ring foot. The entire visual character of the vessel is determined by the thick calcium-rich grey-green celadon glaze rather than painted decoration — an approach characteristic of Southern Song imperial court aesthetics in which aesthetic expression is created through relief, glaze depth, and subtle colour modulation.

The glaze varies from silver-grey to olive-green depending upon illumination angle, producing the famous warmth and translucency traditionally described as 溫潤如玉 (“warm and smooth like jade”). Alternating light and dark horizontal zones follow every wheel-thrown rib around the body, reflecting differences in glaze thickness and cooling behaviour at rib summits versus valleys. The rim shows a slightly darker tone where the glaze becomes thinner (Zi Kou, 紫口), and the exposed ring foot reveals the dark iron-rich stoneware body (Tie Zu, 鐵足).

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis

The vase was examined using the Dronova Gemmological Optical Microscopy (GOM) Protocol combining portable loupe microscopy and digital microscopy at magnifications from 20× to 100×. The investigation focused on the glaze optical microstructure (anorthite crystallisation, bubble population, phase separation), crackle morphology, glaze–body interface, the Zi Kou / Tie Zu complex, glaze banding, and dragon handle junction integrity. Observations were compared with published SEM, EDS, and XRD studies of authenticated Southern Song Guan ware from Laohudong and Jiaotanxia excavations.

The jade-like optical quality of this glaze is not a surface property. It is generated internally by a complex microstructural system: anorthite microcrystals (3–15 μm), trapped gas bubbles (10–30 μm), and nanoscale liquid–liquid phase separation within the CaO–Al₂O₃–SiO₂ glass matrix. These three light-scattering populations together produce the characteristic wenrun ruyu appearance (溫潤如玉) that defines authentic Guan ware.

GOM Documentation — Glaze Microstructure

~60×

Fig. 5. Crackle channel with mineral infill at moderate magnification. The granular glaze surface texture produced by anorthite microcrystals is visible in the glaze fields between cracks. The crack channel shows jagged crystalline walls consistent with stress fracture through a crystal-bearing glaze matrix.

~100×

Fig. 6. Exposed ceramic body at high magnification. Compacted mineral inclusions and vitrified particles characteristic of high-temperature reduction firing. Dense stoneware matrix consistent with Zijin clay (紫金土) used in Hangzhou official kilns. No casting texture or modern body fabric.

1. Wenrun Ruyu Glaze Appearance — 溫潤如玉

The glaze surface exhibits the classical Southern Song jade-like matte quality described as 溫潤如玉 (“warm and smooth like jade”). This appearance is produced by two populations of light-scattering microstructures within the glaze matrix observable under GOM: fine bright particles interpreted as anorthite (CaAl₂Si₂O₈) microcrystals of estimated 3–15 μm, and rounded gas bubbles of approximately 10–30 μm produced during high-temperature firing. Both populations scatter incident light producing the soft non-specular optical quality. Published SEM, EDS, and XRD data for authenticated Guan ware from Jiaotanxia confirm the presence of these microstructural populations.

2. Glaze Banding

Alternating lighter and darker horizontal zones follow every rib of the wheel-thrown body. This banding reflects the differential glaze thickness between rib summits (thinner glaze, darker tone due to greater iron body influence through thinner glaze) and rib valleys (thicker glaze accumulation, lighter and more opalescent). The banding pattern is formed during firing and cooling and cannot be reproduced by surface treatment. It constitutes direct visual evidence of the multi-layer glaze application characteristic of Southern Song Guan production.

3. Dragon Handle Integration

The paired dragon-ear handles with ring loops are covered with the same continuous glaze as the body. GOM examination of the handle–body junction shows no glaze discontinuity, no gap-filling material, and no evidence of post-firing attachment. The handles were applied to the leather-hard clay body before glazing and fired simultaneously. This construction sequence is confirmed by the uninterrupted glaze flow across the junction and the presence of glaze banding that continues coherently from body to handle surface.

4. Zi Kou Tie Zu Complex — 紫口鐵足

Both elements of the classical Guan diagnostic complex are present. The Zi Kou (紫口) presents as a slight purplish-grey darkening at the trumpet mouth rim where the glaze becomes thinner, allowing the iron-rich body colour to influence the glaze tone. The Tie Zu (鐵足) comprises the dark iron-rich foot ring exposed at the base, revealing the Zijin clay stoneware body. The glazed base interior carries a continuous fine crackle network. The morphology of the ring foot is consistent with authenticated excavated material from Laohudong and Jiaotanxia kiln sites.

5. Anorthite Crystallisation and Glaze Microstructure

GOM microscopy reveals the granular glaze surface texture produced by anorthite microcrystal formation during slow controlled cooling of the calcium-alumino-silicate glaze at 1220–1270°C in a reducing kiln atmosphere. The crackle channel walls display a jagged crystalline morphology consistent with stress fracture through a crystal-bearing glaze matrix. Published archaeometric data confirm that Southern Song Guan glazes contain anorthite crystals, residual quartz, trapped bubbles, calcium-rich glass, and nanoscale liquid–liquid phase separation, all contributing to the characteristic jade-like optical system.

Diagnostic Interpretation

The principal diagnostic characteristic of this vase is not simply the colour of the glaze, but the preservation of a complete optical system formed by anorthite crystallisation, trapped gas bubbles, liquid–liquid phase separation, multilayer glaze application, and controlled reduction firing. These microstructural features collectively generate the jade-like visual quality that defines authentic Southern Song Guan ware.

Technological Reconstruction — Manufacturing Sequence

1

Forming

Wheel-thrown body. Dragon handles moulded and applied to leather-hard clay.

2

Glazing

Multiple calcium-rich glaze layers applied over body and handles simultaneously.

3

Firing

Reduction atmosphere, 1220–1270°C. Glaze becomes fluid; banding forms at ribs.

4

Cooling

Controlled slow cooling. Anorthite nucleation and growth. Phase separation. Bubble entrapment.

5

Result

Jade-like optical system: wenrun ruyu (溫潤如玉). Crackle forms on further cooling.

Diagnostic FeatureObservation
Thick multilayer grey-green celadon glaze✓ Present

Tie Zu — 鐵足 (iron foot ring)✓ Present

Bubble-rich glaze microstructure✓ Present

RestorationNone observed

Key Diagnostic Markers

Thick multilayer grey-green celadon glaze (calcium-alumino-silicate)
Wenrun ruyu jade-like matte texture — 溫潤如玉
Glaze banding following wheel-thrown rib structure
Dragon-ear handles simultaneously glazed with body
Zi Kou (紫口) — purplish-grey rim darkening at thinned glaze edge
Tie Zu (鐵足) — dark iron-rich Zijin clay foot ring
Optical evidence of anorthite crystallisation (3–15 μm)
Bubble population (10–30 μm) and phase separation
Age-consistent crackle with crystalline channel walls
Dense iron-rich stoneware body — no casting texture
No restoration detected

Diagnostic Confidence: Very High
